520__a-1: This is a historical architectural blueprint on aged parchment paper showing detailed technical drawings of a large Victorian-era building. The main view displays a longitudinal section (side elevation) of an ornate structure featuring multiple stories, a prominent central tower with a pointed spire on the left side, pitched roofs with gabled ends, numerous windows and doors arranged symmetrically, decorative railings, and supporting columns. The building demonstrates Victorian architectural style with its vertical emphasis and ornamental details. In the upper right corner, there is a smaller cross-sectional detail drawing labeled "Transverse Section" showing the interior spatial arrangement and structural framework. Handwritten annotations appear throughout the document in brown ink, including text at the bottom reading "Longitudinal Section" and "B. D. Brewster & Struthers" with what appears to be an address. Additional notations mark various architectural elements and measurements. The drawing is executed in brown ink on tan-colored paper with some aging and wear visible. A colored reference scale appears on the right edge of the document.

# Architectural Floor Plan Description

This is a historic architectural blueprint showing the second floor of a building designed by F. McEllough & Son, Architects, located at 330 Pine Street. The document is drawn on aged, cream-colored paper with visible stains and wear marks throughout. A color reference scale and measurement ruler appear at the top of the page.

The floor plan is rendered in brown ink with architectural details including room outlines, dimensions, and layout specifications. The second floor contains multiple rooms labeled as bedrooms and bathrooms distributed across the space. Notable features include two prominent "Bedroom" spaces marked with dimensions like "13'6" x 16'8"" in the upper central portion of the plan. The layout shows various smaller rooms, hallways, and what appears to be closets or service areas marked with abbreviations and measurements.

The lower portion of the plan displays additional room divisions with labeled spaces including "Bed Room," "Bath," and other residential spaces. Diagonal lines in several rooms indicate flooring patterns or ceiling treatments. Detailed measurements and construction notes are written throughout the plan in period handwriting.

At the bottom of the blueprint, text reads "Scaled Eight Feet to One Inch" and identifies the architects and address. The overall composition presents a comprehensive view of the floor's spatial organization and dimensions for construction purposes.

# Architectural Floor Plan - First Floor

This is a historical architectural blueprint on aged parchment paper showing the first floor layout of a residential building. At the top of the document is a measurement scale and a color reference bar with sections in navy blue, cyan, green, yellow, magenta, pink, white, and dark gray. The main floor plan features symmetrical design with multiple rooms labeled throughout. 

The central area contains a large "Kitchen" (approximately 18'x16'), flanked by an "Old Ladies Dining Room" on the left (20'x30'6") and a "Childrens Dining Room" on the right (10'x30'6"). A central hallway runs vertically through the middle with staircases marked for access between levels. 

The lower portion of the plan shows additional rooms including "Hall Rooms," "Bed Rooms" of various sizes (ranging from approximately 12'x13' to 18'x18'), a "Master's Room," "Servants" areas, and other domestic spaces. Multiple smaller rooms are marked with letters indicating specific functions or measurements.

Architectural notations appear throughout with room dimensions and directional markers. At the bottom, text reads "Scale: Eight Feet to One Inch" on the left and "B. M'Dougall & Son, Archts. 330 Pine St. S.F." on the right, identifying the architects and their San Francisco location. The plan shows carefully drawn walls in ink with cross-hatching and detailed dimension lines.
